1: wĕlōʾ-yāḵōl yôsēp lĕhiṯʾappēq lĕḵōl hannĭṣṣāḇîm ʿālāyw wayyiqrāʾ hôṣîʾû kāl-ʾîš mēʿālay wĕlōʾ-ʿāmaḏ ʾîš ʾittô bĕhiṯwaddeaʿ yôsēp ʾel-ʾeḥāyw — "Joseph could no longer control himself (lĕhiṯʾappēq) before all who stood before him, and he cried, 'Remove every man from me!'" Lĕhiṯʾappēq (Hithpael infinitive of ʾāpaq, H662, to restrain/control oneself) — the Hithpael reflexive for the effort of self-containment. In 43:31 Joseph controlled himself (wayyiṯʾappaq); now the same verb's negation: he could no longer do it. Judah's substitution speech (44:18-34) broke through his restraint. The clearing of all attendants is deliberate — Joseph will reveal himself in private so the brothers are not shamed before Egyptians (cf. 45:1 wĕhiṯwaddeaʿ yôsēp ʾel-ʾeḥāyw, "as Joseph made himself known to his brothers"). The Hithpael of yāḏaʿ — "he made himself known" — the reflexive of the knowing-verb; an act of self-disclosure.
2: wayyittēn ʾet-qōlô bĕḇeḵî wayyišmĕʿû miṣrāyim wayyišmaʿ bêt parʿōh — "He wept aloud (wayyittēn ʾet-qōlô bĕḇeḵî) so that the Egyptians heard him and Pharaoh's household heard about it." Wayyittēn ʾet-qōlô (he gave his voice — the Hebrew idiom for crying out loudly) — the same idiom for Rachel's weeping in Jer 31:15 (qôl bĕrāmāh nišmaʿ, a voice heard in Ramah). Joseph's weeping is uncontainable — heard through walls, reported to Pharaoh's household. After years of administrative distance and controlled emotion (42:24, 43:30-31), the dam has broken completely.
3: wayyōʾmer yôsēp ʾel-ʾeḥāyw ʾănî yôsēp ha-ʿôḏ ʾāḇî ḥay wĕlōʾ-yāḵĕlû ʾeḥāyw laʿănôt ʾōtô kî niḇhălû mippānāyw — "Joseph said to his brothers, 'I am Joseph (ʾănî yôsēp)! Is my father still alive?' But his brothers could not answer him, for they were terrified (niḇhălû) before him." ʾĂnî yôsēp — three Hebrew words; the shortest, most devastating self-disclosure in Genesis. Two words of identity, one question about his father — his first words after the reveal are for Jacob, not about the brothers' guilt. Niḇhălû (Niphal of bāhal, H926, to be terrified/dismayed) — they are not merely surprised but terror-struck; the man they sold stands before them as the second ruler of Egypt. Mippānāyw (before his face/presence) — the face that Joseph had hidden, the face that he said they would not see without Benjamin (43:3,5), is now the face of their brother.
4: wayyōʾmer yôsēp ʾel-ʾeḥāyw gĕšû-nāʾ ʾēlay wayyiggāšû wayyōʾmer ʾănî yôsēp ʾăḥîḵem ʾăšer-mĕḵartem ʾōtî miṣrāymāh — "Joseph said, 'Come close to me, please (gĕšû-nāʾ).' And they came close. He said, 'I am Joseph your brother, whom you sold into Egypt.'" Gĕšû-nāʾ (Qal imperative plural + nāʾ of nāgaš, H5066, draw near) — Joseph calls his brothers near before the reconciling word. The physical approach precedes the word of grace — he will not speak forgiveness from a distance but draws them into intimacy. The fuller identification — "whom you sold into Egypt" — names the act without accusation; it is stated as fact, not charge, in preparation for the theological reframing of v5-8.
5: wĕʿattāh ʾal-tēʿāṣĕḇû wĕʾal-yiḥar bĕʿênêḵem kî-mĕḵartem ʾōtî hēnnāh kî lĕmiḥyāh šĕlāḥanî ʾĕlōhîm lĕpānêḵem — "Now do not be grieved (ʾal-tēʿāṣĕḇû) or angry with yourselves for selling me here, because God sent me before you to preserve life (lĕmiḥyāh)." Two verbs of negative emotion released: ʿāṣaḇ (H6087, to grieve/hurt/pain) + ḥārar (H2734, to burn/be kindled — the same root as "his anger burned," the idiom for hot rage). Joseph explicitly releases the brothers from both grief and anger about the selling. Lĕmiḥyāh (to preserve life, H4241 — from ḥāyāh, H2421, to live) — the preservation-of-life purpose clause answers Judah's plea in 43:8 (wĕniḥyeh wĕlōʾ nāmût, that we may live and not die). The narrative's telos was always miḥyāh — life. Šĕlāḥanî ʾĕlōhîm (God sent me) — the first of three uses of this sending-formula (vv5,7,8); God is named as the agent of Joseph's transportation to Egypt.
6: kî-zeh šĕnāṯayim hārāʿāḇ bĕqereḇ hāʾāreṣ wĕʿôḏ ḥāmēš šānîm ʾăšer ʾên-ḥārîš wĕqāṣîr — "For two years the famine has been in the land, and there are still five years in which there will be neither plowing nor harvest." The two + five = seven years frames the timeline precisely as Pharaoh's dreams predicted (41:26-27). Joseph provides the calculation as evidence of the plan's divine origin: the famine's duration confirms the interpretation, which confirms the divine planning that brought Joseph to Egypt at exactly the right time.
7: wayyišlāḥēnî ʾĕlōhîm lĕpānêḵem lāśûm lāḵem šĕʾērît bāʾāreṣ ûlĕhaḥăyôt lāḵem liplyêṭāh gĕḏōlāh — "God sent me (wayyišlāḥēnî ʾĕlōhîm) before you to preserve for you a remnant (šĕʾērît) on earth, and to keep you alive as a great survivor band (pĕlêṭāh gĕḏōlāh)." Šĕʾērît (H7611, remnant — what is left after disaster) — the remnant-theology vocabulary that will become central in Isaiah, Jeremiah, and Micah: God preserves a šĕʾērît through judgment. Pĕlêṭāh gĕḏōlāh (a great deliverance/band of survivors, H6413) — the word for those who escape calamity; used for survivors of war, flood, and exile. Joseph's mission is explicitly articulated in the vocabulary of prophetic remnant-theology: he is the instrument of the covenant-line's preservation through catastrophe.
8: wĕʿattāh lōʾ-ʾattem šĕlāḥtem ʾōtî hēnnāh kî hāʾĕlōhîm wayyĕśîmēnî lĕʾāḇ lĕparʿōh ûlĕʾāḏôn lĕḵol-bêtô ûmōšēl bĕḵol-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim — "So then it was not you who sent me here, but God (lōʾ-ʾattem šĕlāḥtem ʾōtî hēnnāh kî hāʾĕlōhîm); and he made me a father (ʾāḇ) to Pharaoh, lord of all his house, and ruler over all the land of Egypt." The theological pivot: "not you… but God." The brothers did send Joseph; Joseph does not deny the human act. But the ultimate agent was divine. This is the mature biblical theology of concursive divine sovereignty: God works through human agency, including sinful human agency, without reducing either the reality of the human act or the sovereignty of the divine purpose (cf. Acts 2:23: "delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God… you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men"). Joseph's three titles — ʾāḇ lĕparʿōh (father/counselor to Pharaoh — a royal advisor title attested in Egyptian records), ʾāḏôn lĕḵol-bêtô (lord of all his house), môšēl bĕḵol-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim (ruler over all Egypt) — summarize the exaltation arc from 41:40-45.
9: mahărû wĕʿălû ʾel-ʾāḇî wĕʾămartem ʾēlāyw kōh ʾāmar bĕnĕḵā yôsēp — "Hurry and go up to my father and say to him, 'Thus says your son Joseph.'" The messenger-formula kōh ʾāmar (thus says) — the prophetic delivery formula; Joseph uses the royal-prophetic speech form to communicate to Jacob. The urgency (mahărû — Piel, hurry) mirrors the famine-urgency that has driven the narrative; now the urgency is joyful: haste to bring good news.
10: wĕyāšaḇtā bĕʾereṣ-gōšen wĕhāyîtā qārôḇ ʾēlay ʾattāh ûḇāneḵā ûḇnê ḇāneḵā wĕṣōʾnĕḵā ûḇĕqārĕḵā wĕḵol-ʾăšer-lāḵ — "You shall live in the land of Goshen and be near me — you and your children and your grandchildren, your flocks and your herds and all that you have." Gōšen (H1657, Goshen) — the fertile Nile delta region allocated to Israel; the name may derive from Egyptian or may be a Semitic place-name. The promise of proximity — qārôḇ ʾēlay (near to me) — is the relational gift at the center of the material provision. Joseph is not only providing food but restoring family presence after twenty-two years of separation.
11: wĕḵilkaltî ʾōtĕḵā šām kî-ʿôḏ ḥāmēš šānîm rāʿāḇ pen-tiwwārēš ʾattāh ûḇêtĕḵā wĕḵol-ʾăšer-lāḵ — "I will provide for you (wĕḵilkaltî) there, for there are still five years of famine, so that you and your household and all that you have do not fall into poverty." Kûl (H3557, Pilpel: to sustain/provide for/contain — to hold someone in provision) — Joseph will "contain" or "sustain" his father; the same verb is used for God providing manna (sustaining Israel in the wilderness) and for Solomon's administration providing for his household. The five remaining famine years create the urgency for immediate relocation.
12: wĕhinnēh ʿênêḵem rōʾôt wĕʿênê ʾăḥî binyāmîn kî-pî haMĕḏabbēr ʾălêḵem — "And behold, your eyes can see, and the eyes of my brother Benjamin, that it is my own mouth speaking to you." Pî haMĕḏabbēr ʾălêḵem (my mouth speaking to you) — an appeal to direct eyewitness testimony: their own eyes confirm they are seeing Joseph, their own ears confirm they are hearing him, not a proxy. The mention of Benjamin (ʾăḥî ḇinyāmîn) — "my brother Benjamin" — is the first time Joseph publicly identifies the kinship; Benjamin's eyes are named alongside theirs as co-witnesses.
13: wĕhiggadtem lĕʾāḇî ʾet-kāl-kĕḇôḏî bĕmiṣrāyim wĕʾēt kāl-ʾăšer rĕʾîtem ûmihhartem wĕhôraḏtem ʾet-ʾāḇî hēnnāh — "Tell my father about all my honor (kĕḇôḏî) in Egypt, and everything you have seen; and hurry and bring my father down here." Kĕḇôḏî (my glory/honor/weight, H3519, from kāḇēḏ) — the root that opened ch43 (kāḇēḏ, the heavy famine). The famine's heaviness and Joseph's honor/glory share the same root — the wordplay traces the arc from famine-heaviness to honor-weightiness. Joseph's glory in Egypt is the evidence that God's hand is in the events; this is what Jacob must be told.
14: wayyippōl ʿal-ṣawwĕʾrê ḇinyāmîn ʾāḥîw wayyēḇk ûḇinyāmîn bāḵāh ʿal-ṣawwĕʾrāyw — "He fell on his brother Benjamin's neck (ʿal-ṣawwĕʾrê ḇinyāmîn) and wept; and Benjamin wept on his neck." Ṣawwāʾr (H6677, neck/throat) — the neck-embrace formula — falling on another's neck — is the biblical idiom for the most intimate reunion embrace: Laban fell on Jacob's neck (29:13), Esau fell on Jacob's neck and wept (33:4). Joseph's embrace of Benjamin is the reunion of the two sons of Rachel — the only two who share both father and mother. The symmetry (wayyēḇk… bāḵāh) — both weeping, each on the other's neck — is reciprocal grief-and-joy embodied.
15: wayyĕnaššēq lĕḵol-ʾeḥāyw wayyēḇk ʿălêhem wĕʾaḥărê kēn dibbĕrû ʾeḥāyw ʾittô — "He kissed all his brothers and wept over them; afterward his brothers talked with him." Wayyĕnaššēq lĕḵol-ʾeḥāyw (he kissed all his brothers) — the kiss of full reconciliation; Joseph initiates the embrace with each brother. Wĕʾaḥărê kēn dibbĕrû (afterward his brothers spoke with him) — the brothers' silence during the revelation (v3: "they could not answer him") is broken only after the kisses and tears. Words are possible only after the physical reconciliation has occurred. The narrator's understatement — "they talked with him" — leaves the content of that conversation to the imagination.
16: wĕhaqōl nišmaʿ bêt parʿōh lēʾmōr bāʾû ʾăḥê yôsēp wayyîṭaḇ bĕʿênê parʿōh ûḇĕʿênê ʿăḇāḏāyw — "The news reached Pharaoh's house: 'Joseph's brothers have come.' It pleased Pharaoh and his servants." Wayyîṭaḇ bĕʿênê parʿōh (it was good in Pharaoh's eyes) — the standard approval formula. Pharaoh's generosity in vv17-20 is politically sensible (Joseph's family settling in Goshen strengthens his administration) and personally gracious. The Egyptian monarch's welcome of the Hebrew family is the beginning of Israel's sojourn in Egypt — which will become the context for the Exodus.
17: wayyōʾmer parʿōh ʾel-yôsēp ʾĕmōr ʾel-ʾaḥeḵā zōʾt ʿăśû ʿimĕsû ʾet-bĕʿîrĕḵem ûlĕḵû bōʾû ʾarṣāh kĕnaʿan — "Pharaoh said to Joseph, 'Tell your brothers, "Do this: load your animals and go back to the land of Canaan."'" Pharaoh takes over the invitation — making it royal command, not merely Joseph's personal wish. This elevates the summons: Jacob is invited not just by his son but by the most powerful ruler in the known world.
18: ûqĕḥû ʾet-ʾăḇîḵem wĕʾet-bêtêḵem ûḇōʾû ʾēlay wĕʾettĕnāh lāḵem ʾet-ṭûḇ ʾereṣ miṣrāyim wĕʾiḵlû ʾet-ḥēleḇ hāʾāreṣ — "Take your father and your households and come to me, and I will give you the best of the land of Egypt, and you shall eat the fat of the land." Ṭûḇ ʾereṣ miṣrāyim (the best of the land of Egypt) + ḥēleḇ hāʾāreṣ (the fat of the land) — two covenant-provision expressions. Ḥēleḇ (H2459, fat/richness/best portion) is the term for the choicest part of a sacrifice; "fat of the land" = its richest produce. Pharaoh is offering what God promised the patriarchs for Canaan — the reversal will be completed in the Exodus when Israel takes the land's ḥēleḇ back to Canaan.
19: wĕʾattāh ṣuwwêtāh zōʾt ʿăśû qĕḥû-lāḵem mēʾereṣ miṣrāyim ʿăgālôt lĕṭapĕḵem wĕlĕnĕšêḵem ûnĕśāʾtem ʾet-ʾăḇîḵem ûḇāʾtem — "And you are commanded: do this — take wagons from the land of Egypt for your little ones and your wives, and bring your father and come." ʿĂgālôt (H5699, wagons/carts — wheeled vehicles) — wagons were an Egyptian technological superiority; Canaan used pack animals. Pharaoh's provision of wagons is a sign of royal investment in the family's migration. The wagons will become the evidence Jacob needs (v27: "when he saw the wagons Joseph had sent").
20: wĕʿênĕḵem ʾal-tāḥōs ʿal-kĕlêḵem kî-ṭûḇ kol-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim lāḵem hûʾ — "Do not be concerned about your belongings, for the best of all the land of Egypt is yours." Wĕʿênĕḵem ʾal-tāḥōs (let not your eyes spare/regret) — literally "let your eyes not grieve over" leaving possessions behind. The royal instruction to travel light anticipates the Exodus command: Israel will leave Egypt but take the wealth of Egypt with them (Exod 12:36). Here the instruction runs the other direction — don't worry about what you leave behind in Canaan, for Egypt's best is yours.
21: wayyaʿăśû-ḵēn bĕnê yiśrāʾēl wayyittēn lāhem yôsēp ʿăgālôt ʿal-pî parʿōh wayyittēn lāhem ṣēḏāh laddāreḵ — "The sons of Israel did so; Joseph gave them wagons, according to Pharaoh's command, and he provided them with provisions for the journey." ʿAl-pî parʿōh (according to Pharaoh's mouth/command) — the royal authorization formula; Joseph executes the command but it is Pharaoh's authority that makes it binding and royal. The wagons and provisions are doubled in source: Joseph's love and Pharaoh's authority.
22: lĕḵullām nātan laʾîš ḥălipôt śĕmālôt ûlĕḇinyāmîn nātan šĕlōš mēʾôt kesef wĕḥāmēš ḥălipōt śĕmālôt — "To each he gave changes of clothes (ḥălipôt śĕmālôt); but to Benjamin he gave three hundred pieces of silver and five changes of garments." Ḥălipôt śĕmālôt (changes of garments/clothing) — clothing as the sign of status, identity, and honor throughout the Joseph narrative. Joseph's coat was taken from him (37:23); his garment became false evidence (39:12); he was clothed in linen at his elevation (41:42). Now he distributes garments to all his brothers — and five to Benjamin (the royal number, echoing the five portions of 43:34). The reversal is complete: the man stripped of his garment now clothes his family.
23: ûlĕʾāḇîw šālaḥ kĕzōʾt ʿăśārāh ḥămōrîm nōśĕʾîm miṭṭûḇ miṣrāyim wĕʿeśer ʾătōnōt nōśĕʾōt bār wāleḥem ûmāzôn lĕʾāḇîw laddāreḵ — "To his father he sent this: ten donkeys loaded with the good things of Egypt, and ten female donkeys loaded with grain, bread, and provisions for his father for the journey." The double ten (ten donkeys + ten female donkeys = twenty) — the twenty pieces of silver for which Joseph was sold (37:28) is echoed in the twenty donkeys of provision sent back. The goods of Egypt flowing to Jacob through Joseph reverse the direction of the transaction that sent Joseph to Egypt.
24: wayyĕšallaḥ ʾet-ʾeḥāyw wayyēlĕḵû wayyōʾmer ʾălêhem ʾal-tirgĕzû badāreḵ — "He sent his brothers off and they departed; he said to them, 'Do not quarrel on the way (ʾal-tirgĕzû badāreḵ).'" Tirgĕzû (Qal imperfect jussive of rāgaz, H7264, to tremble/quake/be disturbed/quarrel) — the word covers a semantic range from physical trembling (earth-quaking) to emotional agitation to interpersonal quarreling. Joseph's parting word is a preemptive peace-command: the guilt about what they did to him is now resolved, but the journey home is long and the brothers may still argue about blame. Rāgaz is used for God's agitation against enemies (Ps 99:1) and for the earth trembling at divine presence (Hab 3:7). Joseph uses the same word to warn against the interpersonal turbulence that guilt and recrimination produce.
25: wayyaʿălû mimmṣrāyim wayyāḇōʾû ʾereṣ kĕnaʿan ʾel-yaʿăqōḇ ʾăḇîhem — "They went up from Egypt and came to the land of Canaan, to their father Jacob." The ascending movement reverses the descents of the narrative: Joseph was brought down to Egypt (39:1), the brothers went down for grain (42:1-3), now they come back up (wayyaʿălû) to Canaan. The ascent signals the direction of covenant-return — up to the promised land.
26: wayyaggĕḏû lô lēʾmōr ʿôḏ yôsēp ḥay wĕkî-hûʾ mōšēl bĕḵol-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim wayyāpāg libbô kî lōʾ-hěʾĕmîn lāhem — "They told him, 'Joseph is still alive (ʿôḏ yôsēp ḥay)! He is ruler over all Egypt!' His heart became numb (wayyāpāg libbô), for he did not believe them." Wayyāpāg libbô (his heart became numb/faint, H6313 pûg, to grow numb/be faint/go cold) — this verb appears only here; it describes the physiological effect of overwhelming news on the heart. Jacob's heart cannot process what he is hearing; the organ that has been grieving for twenty-two years cannot immediately absorb the reversal. Lōʾ-hěʾĕmîn lāhem (he did not believe them) — the same root as ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness) and ʾāmēn: Jacob's heart could not "amen" this news.
27: wayyĕḏabbĕrû ʾēlāyw ʾēt kāl-diḇrê yôsēp ʾăšer dibbēr ʾălêhem wayyarʾ ʾet-hāʿăgālôt ʾăšer-šālaḥ yôsēp lāśēʾt ʾōtô waṯṯĕḥî rûaḥ yaʿăqōḇ ʾăḇîhem — "When they told him all the words of Joseph that he had said to them, and when he saw the wagons that Joseph had sent to carry him, the spirit (rûaḥ) of their father Jacob revived (waṯṯĕḥî)." Waṯṯĕḥî rûaḥ yaʿăqōḇ (the spirit/breath of Jacob revived) — ḥāyāh (H2421, to live/come to life) applied to rûaḥ (H7307, spirit/breath/wind). The same verb used throughout this chapter for life-preservation (lĕmiḥyāh, vv5,7): Jacob's spirit comes alive again — the verb of life that Joseph was sent to effect (v5) now effects itself in his father. The wagons — hāʿăgālôt — are the material evidence; the living-spirit is the theological reality. Jacob sees the wagons and his dead spirit revives.
28: wayyōʾmer yiśrāʾēl raḇ ʿôḏ-yôsēp bĕnî ḥay ʾēlĕḵāh wĕʾerʾennû bĕṭerem ʾāmût — "Israel said, 'Enough! (raḇ) My son Joseph is still alive! I will go and see him before I die.'" Raḇ (H7227, much/many/enough/great) — used here as an exclamation of sufficiency: "Enough! — it is too much! — what more do I need?" The same word used for the "great multitude" and the "many" throughout the OT; here it explodes with emotional release. The narrator uses yiśrāʾēl (Israel, the covenant name) rather than yaʿăqōḇ (Jacob, the personal name) for this declaration — it is Israel-the-patriarch who speaks, not Jacob-the-grieving-father. Bĕṭerem ʾāmût (before I die) — the dying-before clause that has haunted the narrative (44:22,29,31); now reframed as urgency for life-giving reunion rather than grief. Jacob's last recorded words in ch45 are about seeing and living.
